synopsis: Buffettology decodes Warren Buffett's investment philosophy through the lens of his former daughter-in-law, explaining how he identifies businesses with durable competitive advantages and predictable earnings. The book teaches readers to evaluate stocks as partial ownership of businesses, applying Buffett's core criteria for selecting companies likely to generate superior long-term returns.

Build draws on Tony Fadell's decades at Apple and Google—where he led development of the iPod, iPhone, and Nest thermostat—to provide practical guidance on product design, leadership, and career growth. Organized as a series of short essays, the book covers how to craft products people need, build and manage teams, navigate startup challenges, and make consequential decisions.

sources.title: Build: An Unorthodox Guide to Making Things Worth Making - Amazon

synopsis: Building Machine Learning Powered Applications guides practitioners through the full lifecycle of developing a machine learning product, from framing an initial problem to deploying and monitoring a working system. Using a hands-on project throughout, the book covers model selection, iterative improvement, evaluation strategies, and the practical engineering decisions that separate prototype demos from production applications.

sources.title: Building Machine Learning Powered Applications | O'Reilly

synopsis: Building SimCity places Will Wright's urban simulation game within a broad history of computer modeling, tracing its intellectual lineage from early programmable computers through systems dynamics and urban planning theory. Chaim Gingold also chronicles Maxis—the company that published SimCity—and its entanglements with venture capital, Nintendo, and the Santa Fe Institute, revealing the forces that shaped the game's design and Wright's career.

Bullshit Jobs argues that a large and growing share of modern employment consists of roles that workers themselves recognize as socially pointless, and that this phenomenon persists for structural rather than economic reasons. David Graeber categorizes these jobs into five types—flunkies, goons, duct tapers, box tickers, and taskmasters—and examines the psychological harm inflicted on people compelled to perform meaningless work.

Burn examines how the human body actually regulates energy expenditure, challenging popular assumptions that exercise directly increases calorie burn or that metabolism varies widely between individuals. Drawing on fieldwork with the Hadza hunter-gatherers of Tanzania, evolutionary anthropologist Herman Pontzer shows that daily calorie expenditure falls within a constrained range regardless of activity level, and explains the implications for weight management and overall health.

sources.title: Burn by Herman Pontzer | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Call Me Ted is Ted Turner's memoir, recounting his rise from managing his father's billboard company to building a global media empire that included TBS and CNN, the world's first 24-hour news channel. The book interweaves Turner's personal narrative with firsthand accounts from family, colleagues, and rivals, covering his competitive sailing career, ownership of the Atlanta Braves, philanthropy, and the disastrous AOL-Time Warner merger.

sources.title: Call Me Ted | Hachette Book Group

synopsis: Campbell Biology is a comprehensive introductory university textbook covering all major domains of biological science, organized around a framework of central concepts that structure each chapter. The text integrates experimental inquiry, current research, and visual pedagogy to help students understand evolution, cell biology, genetics, physiology, ecology, and the mechanisms connecting biological systems across scales.

Capital and Ideology surveys a millennium of global economic history to argue that inequality is always sustained by political ideologies that legitimize ownership regimes, from feudal societies through colonialism to modern social democracy. Drawing on cross-national data, Piketty proposes a "participatory socialism" grounded in progressive wealth taxation and broader capital access.

sources.title: Capital and Ideology | Harvard University Press

synopsis: Capital Returns compiles reports written by Marathon Asset Management between 2002 and 2015 to illustrate the capital cycle approach to investing, which focuses on how changes in industry supply—driven by capital inflows and outflows—predict future returns. Edward Chancellor's editorial introduction frames the collected reports within a coherent investment philosophy, showing through examples across sectors how monitoring capital allocation can identify mispriced opportunities.

Capital without Borders investigates the profession of wealth management through an eight-year study in which sociologist Brooke Harrington trained and worked as a wealth manager, then conducted interviews across eighteen tax havens. The book reveals how practitioners use offshore structures, trusts, and shell corporations to help ultra-high-net-worth clients shield assets from taxation, creditors, and legal obligations, perpetuating global inequality.

sources.title: Capital without Borders | Harvard University Press

synopsis: John Cassidy's history of opposition to capitalism spans two centuries—from the Luddites and early socialists through anti-colonial movements, feminist economists, and contemporary degrowth advocates—examining what each wave of dissent revealed about the system's failures. The book blends biography and economic theory to show how recurring concerns about inequality, ecological destruction, and technological displacement have shaped the ongoing debate over capitalism's future.

Capitalism in America traces U.S. economic history from colonial origins to the present, arguing that America's exceptional growth stems from its willingness to embrace creative destruction—the displacement of outdated industries by new technologies. Alan Greenspan and Adrian Wooldridge center their analysis on productivity and innovation, asking how policy and culture have alternately accelerated and impeded that dynamism.

Catching Fire picks up after Katniss Everdeen's dual victory in the Hunger Games, following her and Peeta on a mandatory victory tour as President Snow warns that her defiance has sparked rebellion across Panem's districts. The Capitol retaliates by forcing past victors back into the arena for a special Quarter Quell, escalating the conflict between the oppressive government and the uprising gathering around Katniss.

Brian Roberts traces Cecil Rhodes from his English origins to his accumulation of vast wealth in South African diamond mining, his control of De Beers, and his tenure as Prime Minister of the Cape Colony. The biography confronts Rhodes's brutal treatment of indigenous Africans, the Jameson Raid debacle, and the contradictions of a man whose legacy spans the Rhodes Scholarships and the foundations of apartheid.

sources.title: Cecil Rhodes: Flawed Colossus | Publishers Weekly

synopsis: Chaos chronicles the emergence of chaos theory as a scientific discipline, tracing how researchers across mathematics, physics, biology, and meteorology converged on the discovery that simple deterministic systems can produce unpredictable, complex behavior. James Gleick profiles the key figures—including Edward Lorenz, Benoit Mandelbrot, and Mitchell Feigenbaum—whose work revealed hidden patterns in turbulence, population dynamics, and other phenomena previously considered intractable.

Children of Time alternates between the last survivors of a dying Earth searching for a new home aboard a generation ship and the accelerated evolution of an intelligent spider civilization on a terraformed planet intended for humans. When the two species' paths converge, the novel examines whether radically different forms of intelligence can coexist.

Chip War traces the semiconductor industry from mid-twentieth-century military research through Cold War competition to globalized production, showing how microchips became the indispensable foundation of modern economies and military power. Chris Miller then examines the geopolitical contest among the U.S., Taiwan, South Korea, and China for control of chip design and manufacturing.

Civilized to Death argues that the transition from foraging to agricultural civilization introduced chronic stressors—loneliness, overwork, poor nutrition, and ecological destruction—that conflict with biological needs shaped over hundreds of thousands of years of human evolution. Christopher Ryan draws on anthropology and psychology to contend that reconnecting with ancestral social patterns could address many of modernity's most persistent problems.
